trialDescriptions.title: A randomized controlled trial of a senior centre group program for increasing social support and preventing depression in elderly people living in Norway
trialDescriptions.summary:
The aim of the group program was to reach out to elderly aged 65 and over, living at home, with symptoms of loneliness and psychological distress. By having these people participate in senior centre activities one hoped to increase their feelings of social support, alleviate and prevent depression and increase their satisfaction with life. 

The group program consisted of a weekly group meeting of three hours, carried out 38 times in the course of a year. The offer included transport to and from the senior centre if needed, a warm meal, physical training and a self-help group discussing topics participants agreed upon between themselves.
The total number of participants who completed the study was 37 in the intervention group and 55 in the control group. 

There was an increase in social support in both groups, but greatest in the intervention group. For both groups the level of depression increased but more so in the control group than the intervention group. Most partcipants said the intervention meant much to them and led to increased use of the centre. It is recommendable that senior centres expand their activities with group programs strengthening social support. For the depressed, more specialized programs to cope with depression may be a more appropriate intervention.
trialDescriptions.scientificSummary: The objective was to examine the effect of a preventive senior centre group program consisting of weekly meetings, on social support, depression and quality of life in people 65 years and older. A questionnaire was sent to a random sample of 4000 persons over 65 years in Oslo. A total of 2387 completed questionnaires were obtained. These subjects served as a basis for recruitment of participants to a trial. Scores on Hopkins Symptom Checklist (HSCL-10) were used as a main criterion. A total of 138 persons was randomized into an intervention group (n=77) and a control group (n=61). Social support (measured by scores of Oslo-3 Social support scale, OSS-3), depression (measured by scores of Becks Depression Inventory, BDI), life satisfaction and health were measured in face to face interviews prior to intervention and after 12 months. Perceptions of benefits from the intervention were also measured. Mean scores, SD, SE and CI were used to describe the changes in outcomes. Effect sizes were calculated based on the original scales and as Cohen's d. Paired sample tests and ANOVA"one way between groups" were used to test group differences. There was an increase in social support in both groups, but greatest in the intervention group. For both groups the level of depression increased but more so in the control group than in the intervention group. For both groups, there was a decrease in life satisfaction, but the decrease was largest among controls. There were almost no differences in reported health between the groups. The effect sizes were, however, small and differences were not statistically significant. On the other hand, most participants said the intervention meant much to them and led to increased use of the centres. The intervention in all probability failed to meet optimistic targets, but possibly met quite modest ones. It is recommendable that senior centres expand their activities with group programs strengthening social support, but further evaluation of such programs is needed. For the depressed, more specialized programs to cope with depression may be a more appropriate intervention.

observationalGroups.comment: The program consisted of a weekly meeting of 3 hours duration, carried out 38 times during a year at three different senior centres. The groups were fixed and counted 7-10 particpants each. The program embodied transport to and from the senior centre if needed, a warm meal, physical training and a self-help session discussing topics the participants agreed upon themselves. The group leaders were volunteers who had completed a training course for group leaders and they were supervised by the project leader, who was a nurse and experienced leader of the senior centres.

The control group was free to continue daily activities as they chose, and they were offered the same group activities as the intervention group after one year, delayed intervention. No follow-up was conducted.

characteristicDescriptions.primaryOutcome:
One outcome parameter was social support, as measured by scores on the Oslo-3 Social support scale (OSS-3). OSS-3 is based on three questions, with scores on each item and a sum score.
Oslo1: How many people are you so close o that you can count on them if you have great personal problems?
Oslo2: How much interest and concern do people show in what you do?
Oslo3: How easy is it to get practical help from neighbors if you should need it.

Another outcome parameter was depression/psychological distress as measured on the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI).

The participants n=92 were measured at baseline and after six months and again after 12 months after the intervention started.
characteristicDescriptions.secondaryOutcome:
Secondary outcome parametres was:
- life satisfaction, as measured by scores on a question about quality of life
- self-reported health, as measured by scores on a question of health
- after 12 months the intervention group was also asked how musch the weekly program meant to them. They were also asked if they had got new friends or met the participants in private settings.

All these measurements (both primary and secondary) were conducted in an identical manner in face-tofaceinterviews first at baseline and then at 12 months follow-up when intervention had come to an end.

studiedHealthConditionDescriptions.comment: Schwere depressive Episode ohne psychotische Symptome
Severe depressive episode without psychotic symptoms

recruitmentDescriptions.exclusionCriteria: regular users of senior centre, not speaking Norwegian, too mentally or physically ill.
recruitmentDescriptions.inclusionCriteria: Psychological distress, self-reported, not a regular user of the senior -centre, Norwegian speaking, able to communicate in an understandable way, wanted to be a partcipant
